seoTitle: IXYS Technical Support | Power Semiconductor Application Support | LiTong
seoDescription: Technical support for IXYS power semiconductor products including application guides, design resources, and FAE support. Authorized distributor with comprehensive technical services.

faqs.question: How do I verify LiTong's authorized distributor status for IXYS products?
faqs.answer: You can verify LiTong's authorized distributor status through multiple channels: (1) IXYS/Littelfuse official website - check the distributor list on the official website; (2) Authorization certificate - LiTong can provide official authorization documents; (3) Direct verification - contact IXYS directly to confirm LiTong's distributor status; (4) Product authenticity - all LiTong-supplied IXYS products have genuine traceability. As an authorized distributor, LiTong provides full warranty support, technical assistance, and access to IXYS engineering resources.
faqs.decisionGuide: Purchase from LiTong with confidence knowing you are getting genuine IXYS products with full support.

faqs.question: What is the process for requesting a quote?
faqs.answer: Requesting a quote from LiTong is straightforward: (1) Contact information - email sales@litong.com or call our sales hotline; (2) Product details - provide IXYS part numbers, quantities, and any special requirements; (3) Application information - share your application type for appropriate product recommendations; (4) Timeline - indicate your project timeline and any forecasted volumes; (5) Response time - quotes typically provided within 24-48 hours for standard products; (6) Volume pricing - tiered pricing available for volume orders. For complex projects, our FAE team can provide technical consultation alongside the quote.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong sales with your requirements for competitive pricing and technical support.

faqs.question: How can I get technical support for IXYS products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical support for IXYS products through multiple channels: (1) Phone support - call our technical support hotline during business hours; (2) Email support - send technical questions to fae@litong.com; (3) Online resources - access datasheets, application notes, and FAQs on our website; (4) On-site support - available for major customers and complex projects; (5) Design review - our FAE team can review your power supply or motor drive design; (6) Failure analysis - support for any quality issues or returns. Response times vary by priority: critical issues within 4 hours, standard questions within 24 hours.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong FAE team for prompt technical support on IXYS products.

faqs.question: What is the RMA process for defective products?
faqs.answer: LiTong's RMA process for IXYS products: (1) Contact - reach out to support@litong.com with product details and failure description; (2) Troubleshooting - our FAE team will help diagnose the issue remotely; (3) RMA number - if confirmed defective, an RMA number will be issued; (4) Return - ship the defective product to LiTong with RMA number clearly marked; (5) Analysis - IXYS engineering team analyzes the failure; (6) Replacement - replacement shipped within 3-5 business days after receipt. Standard warranty period is typically 2 years. Note: Physical damage and misuse are not covered under warranty.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong support for RMA assistance. We aim to minimize downtime with fast turnaround.

faqs.question: What design resources are available for IXYS products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive design resources for IXYS products: (1) Datasheets - complete electrical and thermal specifications; (2) Application notes - detailed design guidelines for motor drives, SMPS, and power supplies; (3) SPICE models - accurate device models for circuit simulation; (4) Reference designs - proven circuit topologies and PCB layouts; (5) Thermal models - simulation data for thermal design; (6) CAD libraries - footprint and symbol libraries for PCB design. These resources help accelerate your design cycle and ensure successful implementation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Access comprehensive design resources through LiTong to accelerate your IXYS implementation.

faqs.question: How do I select the right power semiconductor for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting power semiconductors involves: (1) Voltage rating - choose at least 20-30% margin above maximum operating voltage; (2) Current rating - based on thermal constraints and RMS current requirements; (3) Switching frequency - MOSFETs for high frequency, IGBTs for lower frequency high power; (4) Thermal requirements - evaluate power dissipation and cooling needs; (5) Package - select based on power dissipation and mounting requirements. IXYS offers MOSFETs, IGBTs, diodes, and thyristors for various applications. Contact LiTong FAE for personalized selection guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider voltage, current, frequency, and thermal requirements when selecting power semiconductors.

faqs.question: What is the typical lead time for IXYS products?
faqs.answer: IXYS product lead times vary by product type and volume: (1) Standard catalog products - typically 8-12 weeks for common configurations; (2) High-volume orders - 10-14 weeks for large quantities; (3) Custom configurations - 14-20 weeks for special requirements; (4) Samples - 2-4 weeks for engineering samples. LiTong maintains strategic inventory for high-demand IXYS products to support urgent requirements. For production programs, we recommend forecasting and buffer stock arrangements to ensure continuous supply.
faqs.decisionGuide: Standard lead time 8-12 weeks; contact LiTong for urgent requirements and volume pricing.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide sample support for IXYS products?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides comprehensive sample support for IXYS products: (1) Engineering samples - available for design validation and testing; (2) Sample quantities - typically 5-10 units for initial evaluation; (3) Sample lead time - 2-4 weeks depending on product; (4) Evaluation kits - reference designs for testing device performance; (5) Technical support - FAE assistance for sample evaluation. Samples are available for qualified customers and projects. Contact LiTong sales with your project details to request samples.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request samples through LiTong sales for design validation and performance testing.

articles.title: IXYS Power MOSFET Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting the right IXYS power MOSFET for your application.
content: Selecting the right power MOSFET is crucial for power electronics design success. This guide walks through the IXYS MOSFET portfolio and provides selection criteria based on application requirements.
IXYS offers both planar and trench MOSFET technologies:
Contact LiTong's application engineering team for personalized selection assistance.

faqs.question: When should I choose planar vs trench MOSFET?
faqs.answer: Choose planar MOSFETs for high-voltage applications (>500V) where avalanche ruggedness is important. Choose trench MOSFETs for low-voltage high-current applications (<200V) where low Rds(on) is critical. Planar devices have better body diode performance and avalanche capability, making them ideal for motor drives with inductive loads. Trench devices offer lower Rds(on) per die area, making them cost-effective for high-current DC-DC converters. IXYS X-Class series uses planar technology for 500V+ applications, while TrenchFET series uses trench technology for 40-200V applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose planar for high-voltage and avalanche ruggedness; trench for low-voltage high-current applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate required voltage rating?
faqs.answer: Calculate MOSFET voltage rating with adequate margin: (1) Determine maximum DC bus voltage including tolerances; (2) Add voltage spikes from switching (typically 20-30% of bus voltage); (3) Include safety margin of 20-30%. Formula: Vds(min) = Vbus_max × 1.3 × 1.3. Example: For 400V bus with 10% tolerance, Vds(min) = 440V × 1.3 × 1.3 = 744V. Select standard rating above this, such as 800V or 1000V. For AC line applications, consider peak voltage (325V for 230VAC) plus transients. IXYS offers MOSFETs from 40V to 1000V+ to cover all application requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 1.7-2x safety factor above maximum operating voltage including transients.

faqs.question: What is the trade-off between Rds(on) and gate charge?
faqs.answer: Rds(on) and gate charge (Qg) present a fundamental trade-off in MOSFET design: (1) Lower Rds(on) reduces conduction losses but typically increases die size and Qg; (2) Lower Qg enables faster switching and lower gate drive losses but may increase Rds(on); (3) High-frequency applications (>200kHz) benefit more from low Qg; (4) High-current applications benefit more from low Rds(on). Modern IXYS MOSFETs optimize both parameters through advanced cell design. For SMPS applications, prioritize Qg for high frequency. For motor drives, prioritize Rds(on) for lower conduction losses at moderate frequencies.
faqs.decisionGuide: Prioritize Qg for high-frequency SMPS; prioritize Rds(on) for motor drives and high-current applications.

faeInsights.summary: MOSFET selection requires balancing electrical, thermal, and cost requirements.
faeInsights.problemAnalysis: Many designers over-specify MOSFETs, leading to unnecessary cost.
faeInsights.solutionApproach: Follow systematic selection process considering actual operating conditions.
faeInsights.insight: The best MOSFET meets all requirements with adequate margins at optimal cost.
faeInsights.logic: Proper MOSFET selection ensures optimal performance and reliability.
keyTakeaways: Consider both conduction and switching losses
Include adequate voltage and current margins
recommendations: Use selection checklists for consistent evaluation
bestPractices: Verify avalanche capability for inductive loads
Match gate drive to Qg requirements
troubleshootingTips: Check gate drive voltage and current

customerCases.problem: Selected MOSFETs with insufficient avalanche rating
customerCases.diagnosis: Motor cable inductance causing voltage spikes above rating
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with IXYS X-Class MOSFETs with 500mJ avalanche rating
customerCases.feedback: LiTong FAE helped us understand avalanche requirements and select appropriate devices.

articles.title: IXYS Power Semiconductor Thermal Design Guide
articles.summary: Best practices for thermal design and heat dissipation in IXYS power semiconductor applications.
content: Proper thermal management is critical for power semiconductor reliability and performance. This guide covers thermal design considerations for IXYS MOSFETs, IGBTs, and diodes.
Power dissipation in semiconductors creates heat that must be removed:
- Junction temperature (Tj) must not exceed maximum rating
- Heat flows: Junction → Case → Heatsink → Ambient
Contact LiTong FAE for thermal modeling support.

author.name: David Liu
author.title: Principal FAE - Thermal Engineering
author.experience: 12 years in thermal design and power electronics

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required heatsink size?
faqs.answer: Calculate required heatsink thermal resistance: (1) Determine total power dissipation P = Pconduction + Pswitching; (2) Determine maximum allowable junction temperature Tj_max (typically 150°C or 175°C); (3) Determine maximum ambient temperature Ta_max; (4) Calculate required Rth(s-a) = (Tj_max - Ta_max)/P - Rth(j-c) - Rth(c-s). Example: P=50W, Tj_max=150°C, Ta_max=50°C, Rth(j-c)=0.5°C/W, Rth(c-s)=0.2°C/W. Rth(s-a) = (150-50)/50 - 0.5 - 0.2 = 2 - 0.7 = 1.3°C/W. Select heatsink with Rth(s-a) ≤ 1.3°C/W under your airflow conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required Rth(s-a) based on power dissipation and temperature limits.

faqs.question: What thermal interface material should I use?
faqs.answer: Thermal interface material (TIM) selection depends on application: (1) Thermal grease - best thermal performance (0.1-0.3°C·cm²/W), requires careful application; (2) Thermal pads - easier assembly, good for production (0.3-0.8°C·cm²/W), various thicknesses available; (3) Phase change materials - combine benefits of grease and pads; (4) Graphite sheets - excellent for high power density. For TO-220 and TO-247 packages, thermal resistance Rth(c-s) typically 0.2-0.5°C/W with good TIM. Apply thin, uniform layer of grease or use appropriate thickness pad. Replace TIM if removing and reattaching device.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use thermal grease for best performance, pads for ease of assembly. Target Rth(c-s) < 0.5°C/W.

faqs.question: How does altitude affect thermal performance?
faqs.answer: Altitude affects air density and cooling effectiveness: (1) Natural convection - reduces approximately 10% per 1000m altitude increase due to lower air density; (2) Forced air cooling - less affected but still degrades at high altitude; (3) Thermal resistance increases with altitude, requiring larger heatsinks or increased airflow. At 3000m altitude, natural convection cooling is reduced by ~30%. Solutions: (1) Increase heatsink size by 20-30%; (2) Increase airflow for forced cooling; (3) Use liquid cooling for high-altitude applications. IXYS devices operate at full rating up to 2000m; derate above this altitude or improve cooling.
faqs.decisionGuide: Increase heatsink capacity 20-30% for operation above 2000m altitude.

faeInsights.summary: Thermal design is often the limiting factor in power electronics performance.
faeInsights.problemAnalysis: Many thermal failures result from inadequate heatsinking or poor TIM application.
faeInsights.solutionApproach: Use conservative thermal design with adequate margins.
faeInsights.insight: Proper thermal design ensures long-term reliability and consistent performance.
faeInsights.logic: Lower operating temperatures improve reliability and reduce parameter drift.
keyTakeaways: Calculate thermal requirements at worst-case conditions
recommendations: Test thermal performance at full load
commonPitfalls: Using datasheet Rds(on) at 25°C for loss calculations
Poor TIM application creating hot spots
bestPractices: Calculate losses at maximum junction temperature
Use forced air for power levels >50W
Apply TIM carefully following manufacturer guidelines
troubleshootingTips: Measure case temperature to verify thermal design
Check TIM contact pattern when removing devices

customerCases.solution: Redesigned with larger heatsink and improved airflow
customerCases.results: Reduced junction temperature by 35°C, improved reliability
customerCases.feedback: LiTong's thermal analysis identified the root cause and helped optimize our design.

articles.title: IXYS MOSFET Gate Drive Design Guide
articles.summary: Best practices for designing gate drive circuits for IXYS power MOSFETs.
content: Proper gate drive design is essential for achieving optimal performance from IXYS power MOSFETs. This guide covers gate drive requirements and design considerations.
1. Turn-on delay - charging input capacitance to Vth
2. Miller plateau - charging Miller capacitance during switching
3. Overdrive - charging to final gate voltage
Contact LiTong FAE for gate drive design support.

author.name: Jennifer Wang
author.title: Senior FAE - Gate Drive Design
author.experience: 10 years in gate drive and power supply design

faqs.question: What gate voltage should I use for IXYS MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Recommended gate voltage for IXYS power MOSFETs: (1) Standard MOSFETs - 10-15V for optimal performance; (2) Logic-level MOSFETs - 5V drive compatible; (3) 10V drive - provides good performance with margin; (4) 12V drive - recommended for most applications, provides good margin; (5) 15V drive - optimal for lowest Rds(on) and fastest switching. Never exceed 20V gate voltage as it can damage the gate oxide. For high-reliability applications, use 12-15V with undervoltage lockout. The gate threshold voltage is typically 2-4V, so 10V+ provides adequate overdrive.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 12-15V gate drive for standard MOSFETs; 5V for logic-level devices.

faqs.question: How do I select the gate resistor value?
faqs.answer: Gate resistor selection balances switching speed and EMI: (1) Lower resistance - faster switching, lower switching losses, but higher EMI and voltage overshoot; (2) Higher resistance - slower switching, reduced EMI, but higher switching losses; (3) Typical values - 5-50Ω depending on switching frequency and application; (4) High-frequency SMPS (>200kHz) - use 5-10Ω for fast switching; (5) Motor drives (5-20kHz) - use 10-22Ω for good balance. Calculate required gate drive current: Ig = Qg × fsw for average current, Ig_peak = Vdrive / Rgate. Use separate resistors for turn-on and turn-off if different speeds needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 5-10Ω for high-frequency SMPS, 10-22Ω for motor drives.

faqs.question: What is the Miller effect and how does it affect switching?
faqs.answer: The Miller effect occurs due to drain-gate capacitance (Cgd) during switching: (1) When drain voltage falls during turn-on, Cgd transfers charge to the gate; (2) This creates a plateau in gate voltage (Miller plateau) where gate voltage stays constant while drain voltage switches; (3) The Miller plateau duration determines switching time and losses; (4) Higher Cgd or higher dV/dt increases Miller effect. Minimize Miller effect by: (1) Using low Cgd MOSFETs; (2) Providing adequate gate drive current during plateau; (3) Using negative gate voltage for turn-off. IXYS MOSFETs are characterized for Cgd and Miller charge to aid design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide sufficient gate drive current to quickly traverse Miller plateau.

faeInsights.summary: Gate drive design significantly impacts MOSFET performance and reliability.
faeInsights.problemAnalysis: Many switching issues stem from inadequate gate drive design.
faeInsights.solutionApproach: Design gate drive for required switching speed with adequate margin.
faeInsights.insight: Proper gate drive ensures efficient switching and reliable operation.
faeInsights.logic: Gate drive must provide sufficient voltage and current for required switching performance.
keyTakeaways: Size gate resistor for switching requirements
recommendations: Include negative gate voltage for high-power applications
Optimize gate resistor for EMI vs losses
commonPitfalls: Gate resistor too large causing slow switching
bestPractices: Use Kelvin source connection for gate drive
Test switching at full load and temperature

customerCases.problem: Excessive switching losses and EMI in 200kHz converter
customerCases.diagnosis: Gate resistor too large causing slow switching
customerCases.solution: Reduced gate resistor from 47Ω to 10Ω and optimized layout
customerCases.results: Reduced switching losses by 40%, EMI within limits
customerCases.feedback: LiTong FAE helped optimize our gate drive design for high-frequency operation.
